text: I’m thrilled by how many zero-carbon solutions are getting ready to scale. The easier clean technologies are to adopt, the faster reliable energy will reach and benefit more people.

Reflecting on my time at the Breakthrough Energy Ventures Investor Summit last week, I’m leaving energized by what I saw. The pace of progress is exceeding even my most optimistic expectations, with breakthroughs occurring and scaling faster than expected. What’s most exciting, though, is the potential impact: These advances could make reliable energy more accessible to people around the world. It’s inspiring to be part of that momentum.

People coming together to build and scale what’s next in energy.

At the Breakthrough Energy Ventures Investor Summit, it was clear as day: reliable, affordable energy is the backbone of opportunity and prosperity.

In the next 25 years, we’ll need nearly three times as much energy as we do today. With breakthroughs in geothermal, fusion, and hydrogen energy, I believe we’ll get there—with options for generating affordable, reliable, clean electricity at a scale the future demands.
With m-mama, motorcycle drivers and boat captains can provide lifesaving transportation for pregnant women when it matters most.
For the past 20 years, (RED) has been a powerful force in mobilizing resources to support the Global Fund’s lifesaving work. Congratulations on 20 years of extraordinary impact.

I’m deeply sad to learn of the passing of Dr. Bill Foege. Bill was a towering figure in global health—a man who saved the lives of literally hundreds of millions of people. He was also a friend and mentor who gave me a deep grounding in the history of global health and inspired me with his conviction that the world could do more to alleviate suffering.
 
Bill and I often talked together about the future of global health. The legacy of his career is that many of the remarkable developments to come will have his imprint all over them.

I’m inspired by the way Indian innovators are harnessing AI to improve lives at scale and advance social good, and I can’t wait to join the conversation at the India AI Impact Summit in New Delhi next month.

The #Goalkeepers2030 event in Stockholm celebrated heroes working on solutions to the world’s toughest challenges. 

When it comes to reducing child deaths, we can’t stop at “almost.” And Nordic leadership,  innovation, and changemakers like these are making sure we don’t.
AI tools are a powerful way to support health workers—helping them extend clinical capacity, improve quality of care, and reach more people, especially in places where resources are scarce.

As we start the new year, I am thinking about how 2026 will position us for the decades to come. The innovation pipeline sets us up for long-term success, but progress over the next decade hinges on how the world addresses three key questions: how to increase generosity, how to scale innovation, and how to minimize disruptions as AI accelerates.
Within the next decade, we will not only get the world back on track but enter a new era of unprecedented progress. The key will be, as it always has been, innovation.
